questions.question: How many data qubits are in an L×L toric code?
questions.explanation: The toric code has 2L² data qubits because there are L² horizontal edges and L² vertical edges on an L×L torus. This is twice as many as the planar surface code of similar dimensions.

questions.question: Why does the toric code encode 2 logical qubits instead of 1?

options.text: It has twice as many physical qubits
The torus has genus 1, giving two independent non-contractible cycles
It uses a different stabilizer structure
The boundaries allow for more encoding

questions.explanation: The torus has genus g=1, which provides two independent non-contractible cycles (horizontal and vertical loops). Each cycle corresponds to one logical qubit's worth of topologically protected information, giving k = 2g = 2 logical qubits.

questions.question: What is the code distance of an L×L toric code?
questions.explanation: The code distance is L, the minimum weight of a logical operator. To flip a logical qubit requires completing a non-contractible loop around the torus, which has minimum length L (either L horizontal or L vertical edges).

questions.question: How many independent stabilizer generators does an L×L toric code have?
questions.explanation: There are L² vertex stabilizers and L² plaquette stabilizers, but two constraints: the product of all vertex stabilizers equals I, and the product of all plaquette stabilizers equals I. This gives 2L² - 2 independent generators.

questions.question: What happens when an error chain forms a complete loop around the torus?

options.text: It is equivalent to a stabilizer (no effect)
If non-contractible, it implements a logical operator without syndrome

questions.explanation: A complete loop around the torus (non-contractible cycle) has no syndrome defects but implements a logical operator, flipping one of the logical qubits. This is a logical error that goes undetected. Contractible loops are just stabilizers with no effect.

questions.question: Compared to a planar surface code with the same distance d, the toric code:

options.text: Uses fewer qubits and encodes fewer logical qubits
Uses more qubits but encodes twice as many logical qubits

questions.explanation: For distance d, the toric code uses 2d² qubits (vs d² for planar) but encodes 2 logical qubits (vs 1 for planar). The encoding rate is the same (k/n), but you get two logical qubits in one code block.

questions.question: The logical X₁ and Z₁ operators of the toric code anticommute because they share an odd number of qubits.
questions.explanation: True. X₁ (vertical X-path) and Z₁ (horizontal Z-loop) intersect at exactly one qubit (where the row and column cross). Since X and Z anticommute on a single qubit, the logical operators anticommute, as required for a logical qubit.
